era_context: Early 14th century: Papal authority under Boniface VIII is challenged by Philip IV of France; the Yuan Dynasty in China faces succession instability; the Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia; the Mamluk Sultanate dominates the Eastern Mediterranean; Mali Empire is rising in West Africa.

events.title: Anagni Incident: Conflict between Pope Boniface VIII and Philip IV of France
events.description: In September 1303, French forces under Guillaume de Nogaret, acting on orders from Philip IV, attacked Pope Boniface VIII at his residence in Anagni, Italy. The assault was a dramatic escalation of the conflict between papal authority and French royal power over taxation of clergy and ecclesiastical independence. Though Boniface was rescued, the incident severely damaged papal prestige and demonstrated the declining power of the papacy relative to emerging nation-states. Boniface died shortly after (October 1303), and the papacy never fully recovered its medieval authority.
events.certainty_note: The Anagni Incident is well-documented in papal records, French royal archives, and contemporary chronicles. The date of September 1303 is secure.

events.title: Death of Pope Boniface VIII
events.description: Pope Boniface VIII died on October 11, 1303, shortly after the Anagni Incident. His death marked the end of an era of papal temporal power and the beginning of the papacy's decline as a major political force in European affairs. Boniface's papacy had been marked by conflicts with secular rulers, particularly Philip IV of France, over ecclesiastical authority and taxation. His death precipitated a succession crisis and eventually led to the election of Clement V and the relocation of the papal seat to Avignon.
events.certainty_note: The date of Boniface VIII's death (October 11, 1303) is well-documented in papal records.

events.title: Battle of Bileçik: Ottoman victory in Anatolia
events.description: Ottoman forces under Osman I defeated Byzantine forces at Bileçik, consolidating Ottoman control over northwestern Anatolia. This victory was part of the Ottoman expansion that would eventually lead to the conquest of Constantinople and the establishment of the Ottoman Empire as a major Mediterranean power. The battle demonstrated the military effectiveness of Ottoman cavalry and the weakness of Byzantine defenses in Anatolia.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman early history is poorly documented; the battle of Bileçik is attested in later Ottoman chronicles but dating is uncertain (1303–1326). Assigned to 1303 by some sources but disputed.

events.title: Mamluk-Ilkhanate tensions in the Levant
events.description: Ongoing military and diplomatic tensions between the Mamluk Sultanate and the Ilkhanate (Mongol state in Persia) characterized the early 1300s. Border skirmishes and raids occurred regularly as both powers competed for control of Syria and Mesopotamia. These conflicts would continue until the Ilkhanate's collapse in the 1330s.
events.certainty_note: Mamluk-Ilkhanate tensions are well-documented but specific events in 1303 are not precisely dated.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty succession disputes following Kublai Khan's successors
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty faced internal instability as various Mongol factions competed for control following the deaths of Kublai Khan's immediate successors. By 1303, the succession was contested, with multiple claimants to the throne. This period of instability would continue until Temür Khan's consolidation in 1307.
events.certainty_note: Yuan succession disputes are documented but specific events in 1303 are not precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Bileçik is traditionally dated to 1303, but modern scholarship disputes this dating; some sources place it as late as 1326. The exact date and significance of this battle remain contested among Ottoman historians. No other significant disconfirming evidence identified for the remaining events.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1303 is moderate but concentrated in European and Islamic sources. The Anagni Incident and Boniface VIII's death are exceptionally well-documented in papal and French royal records. Islamic sources (Mamluk and Ilkhanate) are substantial but often compiled later. Chinese sources exist but are filtered through Yuan imperial historiography. West African sources are sparse. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, cultural, and technological developments are underrepresented. The Anagni Incident is a pivotal moment in European history but may overshadow equally significant developments in Asia and Africa.

graph_edges.note: The Anagni Incident precipitated Boniface VIII's death and the end of his papacy.
Boniface VIII's death led to a succession crisis and the election of Clement V.
